*ADMITTED KANSAS

Jeevan Ahuja
Legal Division
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Letter No. 90-303

Dear Mr. Ahuja:

You have asked that we expand somewhat upon our
original letter, particularly in the area of the discretion
of the special inspectors.

You should be advised that these special inspectors
are typically employed in highly specialized areas of
construction in which city staff have little or no
expertise. These include concrete work, reinforced steel
work, welding, high strength bolting, structural masonry,
reinforced concrete, insulating concrete fill, spray applied
fireproofing, piling drilled piers and caissons, shot creed
and special grading excavation and filling.

Typically a special inspector is an individual with
special qualifications in one of these areas. It is
impossible in most cases for the building official or his
employees to second guess a recommendation made by one of
these special inspectors since City staff lacks the

Jeevan Ahuja
July 13, 1990
Page 2




     inspector's technical expertise.  Therefore, a
recommendation to the building official by a special
inspector is virtually a final decision on the matter.

     Obviously, if the job of the special inspector was
simply to review work to see if it complied with objective
criteria in the Building Code the role of the special
inspector would be ministerial.  However, most special
inspectors exercise judgment with regard to methodology
employed and most also perform calculations extrapolate
from the results of those calculations to determine if
minimum standards are met.  This is rarely as simple as
conducting a simple measurement to determine if, for
example, a bolt has a one-eighth inch thickness.  Such
standards are abstract enough to require a considerable
level of skill on the part of the special inspector in
determining whether or not they are met.

     Because City staff lacks this level of expertise
there is no way to determine if for example the
determination of a welding inspector that the computed
compressive stress, taken alone does not exceed the
applicable standard.  Such determinations can only be made
by a combination of calculation and judgment.  For all
practical purposes such determinations made by a special
inspector are virtually final.

     We are hopeful that this information will be
helpful in allowing you to render an opinion.  Should you
have specific questions or wish to discuss this matter
further please feel free to contact me at anytime.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert V. Wadden, Jr.
                                        for BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN

RVW/LTR51935
cc:  Leland C. Dolley, City Attorney

213-236-0600
OUR FILE NO.

00111-001

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Request for Formal Written Advice

Dear Ms. Donovan:

The Council of the City of El Segundo is requesting
formal advice pursuant to Section 83114(b) of the Government
Code and Section 18329 of the Regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission. I am the City Attorney for
the city of El Segundo. I have been authorized by the City
Council to make this request regarding the Council's duties
under the Fair Political Practices Act and the Health and
Safety Code of the State of California.

BACKGROUND INFORMATION

Cities are required to adopt substantially the same
building standards as contained in the most recent edition
of the Uniform Building Code ("UBC"). The most recent
Uniform Building Code is the 1988 Edition.

Section 305 of the UBC requires that all
construction for which a permit is required shall be subject
to inspection by a building official. Section 306 of the

Kathryn E.Donovan
California Fair Political Practices Commission
April 20, 1990
Page 2


UBC provides that in addition to the general inspections
required of the building official the owner or the owner's
agent shall employ special inspectors to provide inspections
of concrete work, reinforced steel work, welding, high
strength bolting, structural masonry, reinforced concrete,
insulating concrete fill, spray applied fireproofing, piling
drilled piers and caissons, shotcrete, and special grading
excavation and filling.  Copies of Section 305 and 306 are
attached for your convenience.

     These special inspectors appointed pursuant to
Section 306 are employed and paid directly by the owner or
the owner's agent.  They are charged with inspecting the
specialized work described above and certifying it at
various stages of construction.

     Chapter 7 of Title 9 of the Government Code
(Government Code Sections 87100 et. seq.) prohibits public
officials from making or participating in any way in a
government decision in which the official has a financial
interest.  (Government Code Section 87100.)  The term
"public official" is broadly defined by Government Code
Section 82048 as " . . . every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency, . . ."

ISSUES PRESENTED

     1.  Is the special inspector created by Section
305 of the Uniform Building Code, 1988 Edition a "public
official" for purposes of the Political Reform Act?

     2.  If the answer to No. 1 is "yes" does the
special inspector, who is inspecting and certifying work
done on behalf of the person or business entity paying his
salary, have a conflict of interest as described in
Government Code Section 87103?

     3.  If the answers to Nos. 1 and 2 are both "yes"
would the Commission recommend that the City, using its
authority to modify the Uniform Building Code, amend Section
306 to provide that the Building Official will directly
employ special inspectors to perform the requisite
inspections in order to avoid any conflict of interest or
any appearance of a conflict of interest?

Kathryn E.Donovan
California Fair Political Practices Commission
April 20, 1990
Page 3


If you have any questions or if you require
additional information regarding this request, please do not
hesitate to call me. Your assistance in this matter will be
greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,

LELAND C. DOOLEY, City Attorney
CITY OF EL SEGUNDO; and
for BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN

Enclosure

sections.question:
1. Is the special inspector created by Section 305 of the Uniform Building Code, 1988 Edition a "public official" for purposes of the Political Reform Act?

2. If the answer to No. 1 is "yes" does the special inspector, who is inspecting and certifying work done on behalf of the person or business entity paying his salary, have a conflict of interest as described in Government Code Section 87103?

3. If the answers to Nos. 1 and 2 are both "yes" would the Commission recommend that the City, using its authority to modify the Uniform Building Code, amend Section 306 to provide that the Building Official will directly employ special inspectors to perform the requisite inspections in order to avoid any conflict of interest or any appearance of a conflict of interest?

Kathryn E. Donovan  
California Fair Political Practices Commission  
April 20, 1990
sections.facts:
INFORMATION

Cities are required to adopt substantially the same building standards as contained in the most recent edition of the Uniform Building Code ("UBC"). The most recent Uniform Building Code is the 1988 Edition.

Section 305 of the UBC requires that all construction for which a permit is required shall be subject to inspection by a building official. Section 306 of the

UBC provides that in addition to the general inspections required of the building official the owner or the owner's agent shall employ special inspectors to provide inspections of concrete work, reinforced steel work, welding, high strength bolting, structural masonry, reinforced concrete, insulating concrete fill, spray applied fireproofing, piling drilled piers and caissons, shotcrete, and special grading excavation and filling. Copies of Section 305 and 306 are attached for your convenience.

These special inspectors appointed pursuant to Section 306 are employed and paid directly by the owner or the owner's agent. They are charged with inspecting the specialized work described above and certifying it at various stages of construction.

Chapter 7 of Title 9 of the Government Code (Government Code Sections 87100 et. seq.) prohibits public officials from making or participating in any way in a government decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Government Code Section 87100.) The term "public official" is broadly defined by Government Code Section 82048 as ". . . every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency, . . ."

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is the special inspector created by Section 305 of the Uniform Building Code, 1988 Edition a "public official" for purposes of the Political Reform Act?

2. If the answer to No. 1 is "yes" does the special inspector, who is inspecting and certifying work done on behalf of the person or business entity paying his salary, have a conflict of interest as described in Government Code Section 87103?

3. If the answers to Nos. 1 and 2 are both "yes" would the Commission recommend that the City, using its authority to modify the Uniform Building Code, amend Section 306 to provide that the Building Official will directly employ special inspectors to perform the requisite inspections in order to avoid any conflict of interest or any appearance of a conflict of interest?

Kathryn E. Donovan  
California Fair Political Practices Commission  
April 20, 1990
